topics.title: String Algorithms — Sliding Window, Two Pointers & Anagram Patterns
topics.description: String problems are disguised array problems — characters are integers. Core patterns: sliding window for substring constraints (minimum window, longest without repeats), frequency map (int[26] for lowercase) for anagram/permutation checks, two-pointer palindrome check. The sliding window + frequency count pattern solves ~80% of string interview questions. Time complexity target: O(n) or O(n·m) at worst.
topics.story: Strings look intimidating but they're arrays of characters. The 'minimum window substring' problem baffles candidates — until you see it's just a sliding window where you expand right until all target characters are covered, then shrink left to find the minimum. At every step, a 26-element frequency array tells you instantly whether all characters are satisfied. What once looked like O(n²) brute force becomes elegant O(n) with a window and two counters.
topics.analogy: String = array of ints (char values). Frequency map = histogram: int[26] where index 0='a', 25='z'. Sliding window: right pointer expands (adds char), left pointer shrinks (removes char). 'Formed' counter: how many required chars are currently satisfied in the window. Anagram = same histogram. Palindrome = string reads same forwards and backwards. Two pointers: left from 0, right from end, compare then move inward.
topics.memoryAnchor: Char to index: c - 'a'. Frequency array: int[26]. Sliding window formed pattern: need (distinct chars in t), formed (chars with correct count in window). Expand r → check if window satisfied → shrink l. Anagram: sort both strings OR compare int[26]. Longest palindromic substring: expand from center (2n-1 centers). KMP failure function: partial match table for O(n+m) search. StringBuilder for O(n) string building vs + operator O(n²).
topics.firstPrinciples: String concatenation with + in a loop is O(n²) — each + creates a new string. Use StringBuilder. Character frequency comparison: two int[26] arrays compared in O(26)=O(1). Sliding window invariant: window [l,r] maintains some constraint; expanding r adds a char, shrinking l removes it. The 'formed' counter avoids O(26) check per step — increment when a char's frequency in window matches required, decrement when it drops below.
topics.bruteForce: Minimum window: check every substring O(n²). Anagram in string: generate all substrings O(n²). Longest palindrome: check every center with O(n) expansion = O(n²) — still optimal without Manacher's. String reverse: toCharArray + loop.
topics.optimizedApproach: Minimum window: sliding window with formed counter O(n). Anagram: slide int[26] window — add new char, remove oldest char, compare arrays O(1) per step = O(n). Palindrome from center: O(n²) is best practical, Manacher's is O(n) but rarely asked. Reverse words: split + reverse array + join O(n).
topics.whenToUse: Sliding window: any 'longest/shortest substring with property X' question. Frequency map: anagram, permutation, character count constraints. Two pointers: palindrome, reverse, is-subsequence. StringBuilder: any dynamic string building. String.charAt(i) vs toCharArray(): charAt for sparse access, toCharArray for repeated access. Never += in a loop — always StringBuilder.

examples.title: Minimum Window Substring & Anagram Detection
examples.explanation: Minimum window: 'formed' tracks how many distinct required chars are currently satisfied (count in window == count in t). This avoids O(26) array comparison on every step. Expand r until formed==required (window is valid), then shrink l to minimize while still valid. Anagram: fixed-size window = string p's length; slide one step at a time, adding newest char and removing oldest. Arrays.equals(int[26]) is O(26)=O(1).
examples.realWorldUse: Text search: find all occurrences of any permutation of a keyword. Log analysis: find shortest log window containing all required keywords. DevLearner: substring matching in topic search (future feature). Genome sequencing: find minimum DNA window containing all required nucleotides.

examples.title: Palindrome Checks & String Reversal Patterns
examples.explanation: Palindrome two-pointer: skip non-alphanumeric, compare case-insensitively, converge. O(n). Expand-from-center: 2n-1 centers (n odd + n-1 even). Each expansion is O(n) worst case = O(n²) total — optimal for most interviews (Manacher's O(n) is rarely asked). StringBuilder avoids O(n²) string concatenation in reverseWords. Subsequence: single pass through t with si tracking match progress.
examples.realWorldUse: Input validation: palindrome check for IDs, part numbers. Search: anagram matching for autocomplete. Diff algorithms: LCS (longest common subsequence) for git diff, word processors. Compression: run-length encoding relies on repeated character detection.

problems.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters
problems.description:
Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters.

**Example 1:**
lengthOfLongest("abcabcbb") => 3 ("abc")
lengthOfLongest("bbbbb") => 1
lengthOfLongest("pwwkew") => 3 ("wke")

**Key insight:** Sliding window + HashMap storing last index. Expand right; shrink left when duplicate found.
problems.pattern: Sliding window, HashSet or last-seen map
problems.hint1: Use a HashMap to store last seen index of each character.
problems.hint2: Right pointer expands. If s.charAt(r) was seen at index >= l, jump l to lastSeen[c]+1.
problems.hint3: Track maxLen = max(maxLen, r - l + 1) after each step.

problems.title: Valid Anagram
problems.description:
Check if two strings are anagrams (same characters, same frequencies).

**Example 1:**
isAnagram("anagram", "nagaram") => true
isAnagram("rat", "car") => false

**Key insight:** Sort both and compare, or use a frequency count array of 26 letters (increment for s, decrement for t).
problems.hint1: If lengths differ, return false immediately.
problems.hint2: Count frequency in s with int[26], decrement for t. Any non-zero value means not an anagram.
problems.hint3: Alternative: sort both strings and compare. O(n log n) but simpler.

problems.title: Group Anagrams
problems.description:
Group an array of strings by their anagram class.

**Example:**
groupAnagrams(["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"])
=> [["eat","tea","ate"],["tan","nat"],["bat"]]

**Key insight:** Sorted string as HashMap key. All anagrams share the same sorted form.
problems.hint1: Anagrams have the same sorted string. Use sorted string as HashMap key.
problems.hint2: Map<String, List<String>>: key=sorted word, value=list of original words.
problems.hint3: Iterate words, sort each, add to map under that key. Return map.values().
